breaking.text: Brent crude surges 8.8% to $92.93 on escalating Iran conflict — energy stocks rally while broader market whipsaws
February nonfarm payrolls plunge by 92,000 — unemployment rises to 4.4%, worst jobs report in years
Fed Chair Powell calls economy 'stable and resilient' — sparks midday market reversal, S&P 500 recovers from -1.3% to +0.6%
Broadcom surges 8% on blowout earnings — AI revenue up 77% YoY to $4.1B
February payrolls shock: economy lost 92,000 jobs, worst print since early pandemic

summary: Markets staged a dramatic midday reversal on March 6 after Fed Chair Powell characterized the economy as stable, recovering from steep morning losses driven by a shocking February jobs report showing 92,000 jobs lost and surging oil prices amid the Iran conflict. The S&P 500 clawed back from a 1.3% drop to trade up 0.6% near 5,770, with the Nasdaq leading at +0.7% fueled by semiconductor strength. Broadcom surged 8% on blockbuster AI revenue while Marvell jumped 22% on record earnings, contrasting with Costco's 6.1% decline on an earnings miss and HPE's 12% plunge on weak guidance. The energy sector was a standout as Brent crude spiked to $92.93, lifting Occidental Petroleum 3.3% and Exxon and Chevron over 1% each.

highlights.text: Costco (COST) drops 6.1% and HPE plunges 12% on earnings disappointments, while Walgreens jumps 7.5% on Sycamore Partners take-private deal
Brent crude surges 8.8% to $92.93 on escalating Iran conflict — energy stocks rally with Occidental Petroleum up 3.3%, Exxon and Chevron each gaining over 1%
Day One Biopharmaceuticals soars 65.6% after Servier announces $2.5 billion acquisition for its glioma drug Ojemda
Broadcom (AVGO) surges 8% after reporting AI revenue of $4.1 billion, up 77% year-over-year, in a blowout quarterly earnings report
February nonfarm payrolls plunged by 92,000 jobs with unemployment rising to 4.4% — the worst monthly jobs reading in years and far below the +50K consensus
Fed Chair Powell's midday remarks calling the economy 'stable and resilient' triggered a sharp market reversal — S&P 500 swung from -1.3% to +0.6%
Energy sector stands out as safe haven — Occidental Petroleum (OXY) up 3.3%, Exxon and Chevron each gain 1%+ as oil prices spike on Middle East tensions
Costco (COST) slides 6.1% after quarterly profits miss expectations, citing higher operational costs
Hewlett Packard Enterprise (HPE) plummets 12% on disappointing earnings outlook and announcement of workforce reductions
Walgreens Boots Alliance (WBA) jumps 7.5% on reports it has reached an agreement to go private with Sycamore Partners
Broadcom (AVGO) surges 8% after reporting AI-related revenue of $4.1B, up 77% year-over-year, in a blowout quarterly earnings report
February nonfarm payrolls plunged by 92,000 jobs — unemployment rose to 4.4%, stoking recession fears before Powell's calming remarks
Fed rate-cut expectations pushed back to October as energy-driven inflation and labor market weakness create a stagflationary dilemma — 97% odds of a March hold
Servier agreed to acquire Day One Biopharmaceuticals for $2.5B ($21.50/share, 68% premium) to expand its rare oncology portfolio, sending DAWN shares up 65%
Marvell Technology rallied 12% premarket after posting record fiscal 2026 revenue of $8.2B (+42% YoY), driven by surging AI data center demand
Broadcom beat Q1 estimates with $19.3B revenue (+29% YoY), AI chip revenue of $8.4B (+106% YoY), guided Q2 to $22B and announced a $10B buyback
WTI crude surged 6.3% to $86.06 and Brent hit $89.23 — both at 52-week highs — as the Iran conflict disrupts Strait of Hormuz traffic; Qatar warns crude could reach $150
February nonfarm payrolls plunged by 92,000 — the third jobs loss in five months — as healthcare (-28K from strikes), manufacturing (-12K), and federal government (-10K) all contracted sharply
Costco dips despite strong Q2 results in classic sell-the-news reaction; Walmart up 14% YTD as defensive safe-haven play
Fed pivot from rate-cut talk to 'data-dependent pause' — no relief expected before June at earliest as core CPI remains stubbornly above 2.5%

summary_history.text: Markets staged a dramatic midday reversal on March 6 after Fed Chair Jerome Powell characterized the economy as stable and resilient, calming nerves rattled by a shockingly weak February jobs report that showed nonfarm payrolls falling 92,000 — far below the 50,000 growth expected — while unemployment ticked up to 4.4%. The S&P 500, which plunged as much as 1.3% at the open on the jobs data and rising oil prices from the widening Middle East conflict, has rebounded to trade up 0.6% near 5,770, led by a tech and semiconductor rally. Broadcom's blowout earnings (+8%, AI revenue up 77% YoY) and a Marvell analyst upgrade are powering the Nasdaq's 0.7% gain, while Costco (-6.1% on earnings miss), HPE (-12% on weak guidance and layoffs), and oil-sensitive financials like Goldman Sachs (-3.7%) weigh on the Dow.

U.S. stocks are under heavy pressure Friday morning as a disastrous February jobs report — the economy lost 92,000 jobs versus expectations for a 60,000 gain — collides with surging oil prices driven by the escalating U.S.-Iran conflict now in its seventh day. WTI crude has spiked above $86 and Brent is approaching $90 as the Strait of Hormuz faces near-total shipping disruption, with Qatar's energy minister warning prices could hit $150. S&P 500 futures fell roughly 0.5% premarket while the Fed rate-cut timeline has been pushed to October, with a 97% probability the central bank holds rates at its March meeting. On the earnings front, Broadcom beat Q1 estimates with $19.3B revenue and 106% AI revenue growth, while Marvell Technology rallied 12% premarket on strong AI-driven results.

U.S. stock futures are under pressure Friday morning with S&P 500 futures down 0.29%, Nasdaq 100 futures off 0.39%, and Dow futures declining 0.21% as markets brace for the February employment report amid an escalating Middle East crisis. The Iran-Israel conflict has entered its seventh day with Israel's 'Operation Epic Fury' targeting Tehran directly, triggering closure of the Strait of Hormuz and sending crude oil surging past $81/barrel — its highest level since mid-2024. Thursday's session saw the Dow plummet 784 points (1.6%) to 47,954 while the S&P 500 fell 0.6% to 6,831, failing at 6,900 resistance and erasing all 2026 gains. Investors now await the jobs report at 8:30 AM ET, where economists expect a sharp deceleration to just 60,000 new jobs, complicated by winter weather and a Kaiser Permanente healthcare workers' strike.

U.S. stock futures are trading lower Friday morning with S&P 500 futures down 0.29%, Nasdaq 100 futures off 0.39%, and Dow futures declining 0.21% as investors brace for the February employment report amid escalating Middle East tensions. Thursday's session was brutal — the Dow plunged 784 points (1.6%) to 47,954 while the S&P 500 fell 0.56% to 6,830.71 as crude oil surged over 8% on Iran's claim of striking an American oil tanker in the Persian Gulf. The February jobs report, due at 8:30 AM ET, is expected to show just 60,000 jobs added versus 130,000 in January, with the Kaiser Permanente strike affecting roughly 30,000 positions potentially distorting the data. Bright spots remain in semiconductor earnings with Broadcom jumping 4.8% after hours on a profit beat and Marvell projecting fiscal 2027 revenue approaching $11 billion, though Nvidia faces headwinds from potential AI chip export licensing restrictions.

S&P 500 futures are stabilizing Friday morning after a volatile week dominated by the U.S.-Israeli conflict with Iran that sent oil prices surging and pushed the index negative for 2026. The S&P closed Thursday at 6,830.71 (down 0.56%) after failing to break through 6,900 resistance, with the VIX jumping to 22.08 — its highest since autumn 2025. WTI crude settled at $81.01 Thursday after an 8.5% single-day spike but is pulling back to $80.45 as the Trump administration signals intervention on energy prices. All eyes now turn to the February jobs report at 8:30 AM ET, where economists expect just 50,000 new payrolls — a weak print that could further push back already-diminished Fed rate cut expectations.

Markets are cautiously optimistic Friday morning after a volatile week driven by the U.S.-Israeli conflict with Iran that sent oil prices surging over 14% and erased the S&P 500's year-to-date gains. The S&P 500 closed Thursday at 6,830.71 (-0.56%) with the Dow dropping 785 points as WTI crude settled at $81.01, its biggest weekly gain since 2022, though prices eased overnight after Washington signaled intervention. Broadcom delivered a blowout Q1 report after the bell — revenue hit a record $19.3B (+29% YoY) with AI revenue of $8.4B (+106%), and CEO Hock Tan projected $100B in AI chip revenue by 2027. All eyes now turn to the February nonfarm payrolls report due at 8:30 AM, where consensus expects just 50,000 jobs added, potentially depressed by a Kaiser Permanente strike that affected 31,000 workers during the survey week.

U.S. markets are bracing for Friday's critical February jobs report after a volatile week dominated by the Iran-Strait of Hormuz crisis that has sent oil prices surging nearly 14% in a week. The S&P 500 closed Thursday at 6,830.71, down 0.56%, after failing to breach 6,900 resistance — erasing its 2026 gains. Broadcom provided a bright spot with blowout Q1 earnings, reporting AI revenue of $8.4 billion (up 106% YoY) and projecting $100 billion in AI chip revenue by 2027. Overnight futures point to a modest recovery, with prediction markets giving 65% odds of a higher open, though airline and industrial stocks remain under heavy pressure from energy costs.

Markets are attempting to stabilize Friday after a punishing week driven by the U.S.-Israeli conflict with Iran that began February 28, which has sent oil prices surging 20% and disrupted shipping through the Strait of Hormuz. The Dow plunged 785 points (1.6%) on Thursday while the S&P 500 fell 0.56% to 6,830.71, failing to hold the 6,900 resistance level and slipping into negative territory for 2026. After hours, Broadcom delivered a bright spot with record Q1 revenue of $19.3B and AI semiconductor revenue doubling to $8.4B, while announcing a $10B buyback program. All eyes now turn to the February nonfarm payrolls report due Friday morning, with expectations for just 50,000 jobs added and unemployment holding at 4.3%, as traders price in less than one-in-three odds for a June Fed rate cut.
